title: kernel-utils: hacking on Linux kernels from a Mac
text:
When you do kernel CTF or exploit-dev work, most of your time is spent on the same handful of chores: get a kernel building in a clean environment, boot it under QEMU with the right hardening flags, and wire up a debugger and a way to push your exploit into the guest. Doing this on a Mac adds friction, since the kernel build really wants a Linux toolchain.
kernel-utils is a small toolkit I put together to make that loop fast and repeatable from a macOS host. It installs three commands on your $PATH.

title: kernel-utils: hacking on Linux kernels from a Mac
text:
The three commands

kdev [path] — builds and drops you into a kernel-build Docker container, mounting your current directory (or path) at /work. This is where the Linux toolchain lives, so the actual make happens here.
krun [bzImage] [initramfs] — boots a kernel under QEMU with hardened, CTF-friendly settings: SMEP/SMAP/KPTI on, a gdbstub listening on :1234, and guest port 8000 forwarded to host 8080. It auto-discovers arch/x86/boot/bzImage, ./bzImage, and ./initramfs.cpio.gz in the current directory, so usually you just type krun.
kbuild-initramfs — builds an initramfs.cpio.gz in the current directory, copying files from your cwd into /exploit inside the image.

title: kernel-utils: hacking on Linux kernels from a Mac
text:
A typical session
mkdir ~/kernel-work && cd ~/kernel-work
kdev                                   # enter container, $PWD mounted at /work
# inside the container:
git clone --depth 1 -b v6.17 \
  git://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/stable/linux.git
cd linux
make defconfig
make -j$(nproc) bzImage
krun                                   # boots arch/x86/boot/bzImage
Once the guest is up, pulling in your exploit is a one-liner (the host’s QEMU forward is reachable from inside the guest at 10.0.2.2:8000):
rm exploit; wget http://10.0.2.2:8000/exploit && chmod +x ./exploit

title: kernel-utils: hacking on Linux kernels from a Mac
text:
Knobs worth knowing
A couple of environment variables tune krun:

KU_NIC — the QEMU NIC model. e1000 (default) or virtio (alias for virtio-net-pci). Only pick virtio if your kernel has CONFIG_VIRTIO_NET=y and CONFIG_VIRTIO_PCI=y, otherwise eth0 won’t show up in the guest.
KASLR — 0 (default) appends nokaslr to the kernel cmdline, which is the CTF-friendly default. Set KASLR=1 to enable it.

KU_NIC=virtio KASLR=1 krun

title: kernel-utils: hacking on Linux kernels from a Mac
text:
Install
pipx install .          # or: pip install -e .
That’s the whole thing — three commands that turn “I want to debug a kernel bug” into about a minute of setup instead of an afternoon of yak-shaving.

title: Sending WhatsApp messages with Frida from a Mac
text: This was a mobile reverse-engineering challenge: take the WhatsApp macOS app and drive it from the outside so you can send messages programmatically, without ever clicking anything in the UI. This post walks through how that works and explains the jargon as it comes up — so even if you’ve never reversed an app before, you can follow along.

title: Sending WhatsApp messages with Frida from a Mac
text:
The tools and terms
A few things to know up front:

Frida is a dynamic instrumentation toolkit. In plain terms, it lets you inject your own JavaScript into a running program and call the program’s own functions, read its memory, and intercept its behavior while it runs. It’s the Swiss-army knife of app reversing.
Mac Catalyst is the technology that lets an iPad app run on macOS. The WhatsApp desktop app here is a Catalyst build, which means under the hood it’s really the iOS app — same internal class names, same frameworks.
Objective-C runtime. WhatsApp is written largely in Objective-C, a language where method calls are resolved at runtime by name. That’s a gift for reversers: Frida can look up any class and call its methods by their string names, almost like the app handed us an API.
JID (Jabber ID) is WhatsApp’s internal address for a chat. WhatsApp’s messaging is built on XMPP (the same protocol behind Jabber chat), and every conversation — a person or a group — is identified by a JID that looks like an email address, e.g. 65614920028283@lid. When we send a message, the JID is who we’re sending it to.

title: Sending WhatsApp messages with Frida from a Mac
text:
The plan
We don’t want to reimplement WhatsApp’s encryption or network protocol — that’s a losing battle. Instead we let the real app do all the heavy lifting and just ask it, from the inside, to send a message for us. With Frida injected, we can reach into the app, grab the objects it already uses to send messages, and call the send method ourselves.

title: Sending WhatsApp messages with Frida from a Mac
text:
The catch: thread context
The first naive attempt crashes the app instantly with a SIGABRT (an abort signal — the program intentionally killing itself because an internal sanity check failed).
The reason is that WhatsApp’s send code asserts it’s running inside one of the app’s own work queues — a specific background thread the app uses to serialize its messaging work. (A dispatch queue is just a managed line of tasks that run one at a time on a background thread.) Call the send function from the wrong thread and the app notices the missing context and aborts to protect itself.
The fix is to not fight it. WhatsApp exposes a callback, runWhenConnected:, that hands a block of code to the connection’s own work queue and runs it there. By wrapping our send in that callback, our code runs in exactly the context the app expects — the assertion passes, nothing crashes, and we can fire a message on demand.

title: Sending WhatsApp messages with Frida from a Mac
text:
Driving it
Everything is wrapped in a small interactive Python shell that launches or attaches to WhatsApp, injects the Frida agent, and drops you into a prompt:
# interactive shell
python3 wa_shell.py

# or one-shot from the command line — send one message and exit
python3 wa_shell.py --send "65614920028283@lid" "hello from frida"
Inside the shell, the helpers read almost like a chat API:

send_text(jid, text) — send a text message to the chat identified by jid
send_image(jid, path, caption) — send an image
set_jid(jid) — filter live output to a single chat
status() — sanity-check that the internal send objects resolved correctly

So sending a message is just:
send_text("65614920028283@lid", "sent straight from Frida")
A couple of quirks worth knowing:

The jid is that internal chat address from earlier — not a phone number. The @lid suffix is one of WhatsApp’s addressing schemes for an identity.
Text sending only works for a chat that already has an existing conversation, because the code looks up an existing chat session (the established cryptographic conversation state with that contact) rather than starting a fresh one.
Images need to be “armed” once — send a single image from the UI first — before the programmatic image path will work.

title: Sending WhatsApp messages with Frida from a Mac
text:
Takeaway
The recurring lesson in app reversing: you rarely need to beat the app at its own game. It already knows how to encrypt, address, and send a message — your job is just to find the right internal lever and pull it. And when the app fights back with self-protective assertions, the cleanest move is to borrow its own machinery (here, its work queue) rather than work around it.
